name: Beneath the Bramblewood
notes:
Willowmere is a small, quiet town at the edge of a forest that has forgotten how to be alive. The birds don't sing anymore. The children keep bringing home strange glowing mushrooms from the treeline. The woman who runs the tavern hasn't slept in two weeks. Nobody knows why, but everyone feels it — something is wrong in the Bramblewood, and whatever it is, it's getting closer.
Beneath the roots of the oldest tree in the forest, a spirit named Mira has been sleeping for a very long time. She didn't mean to. She was the heart of the Bramblewood — the reason the moss grew soft and the fireflies came out in summer and the foxes knew which paths were safe. When she fell into her cursed sleep, the forest didn't die. It just slowly started forgetting what it was supposed to be.

areas.name: The Drowsy Badger
areas.notes:
Type: Tavern / Inn
Atmosphere: Welcoming

The smell of roasting meat and spilled ale greets you before you even open the door. Inside, a gruff barkeep wipes down the counter while a bard in the corner picks out a familiar tune on a battered lute.

Notable Features:
A cellar entrance behind the bar that the owner discourages questions about

Encounters / Hazards:
A stranger in the corner

Lore:
Local stories claim something was buried here that was never meant to be found again.

areas.name: Town Square
areas.notes:
Type: Town
Atmosphere: Welcoming

Moss and climbing vines have done their patient work here — the walls are half-swallowed, the towers broken, the floor a cracked mosaic barely visible through years of overgrowth.

Notable Features:
A cellar intact beneath the rubble, accessible through a collapsed section

Encounters / Hazards:
1d8 Skeletons (CR 1/4) animated by residual magic

Lore:
A treaty was once signed here that has since been broken by all parties involved.

areas.name: Old Wren's Hovel
areas.notes:
Type: Other
Lives just outside town in a cottage covered in drying flowers. Ancient, possibly. Speaks in a slow, certain way that makes everything sound important. She knows the Bramblewood better than anyone.

“The forest isn’t angry. It’s afraid. There’s a difference.”

She knows about the mushrooms and the humming. She will not go into the forest herself. She knows why.

areas.name: The Blacksmiths
areas.notes:
Type: Building
Atmosphere: Oppressive
It was a significant structure once. The stonework is fine — you can still see the chisel marks of skilled craftsmen — but time and something else have conspired to lay it low.
Environment & Hazards:
A sealed vault door with a combination lock of symbols

areas.name: The Dark Heart
areas.notes:
Type: Forest Clearing
Ancient enormous trees. Roots erupting from the ground like frozen waves. No mushrooms — that absence should feel wrong. No sound at all. A massive oak at the center with a crack down its trunk glowing faint red from far below.

The Stranger’s camp is here. Cold fire. Same boot prints. Scratched into the bark: “Don’t wake her until you’re ready.”
Environment & Hazards:
The Bramble Guardian — Treant (reskinned) | CR 5 | AC 13

A creature of twisted branches and old roots blocking the crack in the tree. Not a villain. It asks three questions:

Have you helped someone today?
Have you been afraid and done it anyway?
Do you promise to be careful down there?
Answer honestly — it steps aside and gives the party the root bracelet. Attack it — it wins, but always offers another chance to just answer the questions.

“She made me. I miss her. Bring her back if you can.”

areas.name: The Mushroom Maze
areas.notes:
Type: Forest Clearing
Glowing mushrooms grow as tall as the players. The path splits constantly and looks identical every direction. Navigation is impossible by normal means.

The trick: hum or sing and the mushrooms pointing toward the center glow brighter. Old Wren would know this. Pip would accidentally discover it. Let the youngest player lead here. Her voice literally guides the party forward.
Environment & Hazards:
Encounter: The Spore Sprites

Three tiny fae, mischievous but not evil, redirecting travelers away from the center because they’re scared too. They speak in rhymes, steal small objects, and are won over with food — Marta’s muffins work perfectly. Once befriended they give the party a glowing seed that grows the bridge in Chamber Two. Use Pixie stat block, CR 1/4. They flee if combat starts.

areas.name: The Sunny Edge
areas.notes:
Type: Wilderness
Atmosphere: Bustling
Fireflies even during the day. A small creek with smooth stones. A hollow log big enough to crawl through. Pip knows this part.
Environment & Hazards:
The party finds their first real clue here: a child’s wooden toy, half-buried, that doesn’t belong to anyone

areas.name: The Hall of Echoes
areas.notes:
Type: Dungeon
A long corridor lined with alcoves, each containing a stone animal — a fox, a deer, a bird, an owl. Their eyes follow the party. Not threatening. Curious. Talk to them — the torches brighten. Rush past ignoring them — a low rumble, torches dim. This chamber teaches the youngest player that kindness is the mechanic down here, not combat.
Encounter Setup:
The five bats roost in the ceiling: Squeaky (loudest), Noctis (hangs in front of faces and stares), Thistle (slightly sticky fur, no one knows why). They trigger as a Swarm of Bats, CR 1/4, only if the party is loud and sudden. Disperses after one round automatically.

areas.name: The Flooded Room
areas.notes:
Type: Dungeon
Knee-deep perfectly still black water full of drifting stars — not reflection, actual tiny lights. Beautiful and eerie. The floor drops suddenly in the center. Too deep to cross.

Plant the glowing seed in a crack in the wall — it grows instantly into a root bridge arching across the deep section.
Encounter Setup:
Luminara — Ochre Jelly | CR 2 | AC 8 | HP 45 (6d10+12)

Luminara was not always ochre. Centuries of absorbing the starlit water — soaking in Mira’s dreaming light — stained her slowly gold, then amber, then the deep warm ochre she is now. She is the oldest thing in the catacombs besides Mira and Cobweb, and she moves like she knows it. Enormous, slow, completely unbothered.

She doesn’t sting. She engulfs — she just doesn’t always notice what’s inside her. Avoid her by moving slowly and hugging the walls. If hit with slashing damage she splits into two dimmer confused pieces that drift apart. Moving quickly past her triggers a pseudopod opportunity attack: 2d6 acid.

Vex and Drift are also here — smaller jellyfish, ambient. Vex bumps into fast movers (1d4 lightning, purely reflexive). Drift is the size of a dinner plate and harmless. Move calmly and both part like a curtain.

Snip takes a shortcut and falls in the deep section. Fuzz pulls him out using a spoon as a reaching tool. This should not work. It does. This is their heroic moment.

areas.name: The Puzzle Room
areas.notes:
Type: Dungeon
Circular room. Locked stone door. Mosaic floor showing the entire Bramblewood. Five stone pedestals: flame, leaf, water, paw print, heart.
Encounter Setup:
Large spider, bigger than a cat, smaller than a dog. Has lived here so long her webs are woven into the mosaic in genuinely beautiful patterns. Not aggressive. She hatched from an egg in this room before Mira fell asleep and is the last creature Mira spoke to before the long sleep. She has been keeping this room clean and waiting for someone to come solve it for a very long time.

Be kind to her — she climbs onto the heart pedestal and taps it with one leg. She follows the party to the final chamber and sits on Mira’s cradle when she wakes. That is where she belongs.

Puzzle Solutions:

Heart → root bracelet from the Guardian

Paw print → the fox steps on it on its own, right when the party is stuck. The fox knew.

Flame → torch or fire cantrip

Leaf → any natural item carried from the forest

Water → water carried from the flooded room, or water cantrip

The door opens with a sound like wind through branches and the smell of something green and living rushes out.

areas.name: The Sleeping Grove
areas.notes:
Type: Dungeon
A vast underground cavern. Domed ceiling covered in carved stars. Floor of soft moss. Complete silence. In the center — a stone cradle covered in vines, with a figure sleeping inside.
Encounter Setup:
Mira of the Roots — Forest Spirit | CR 2 | AC 13 | HP 50

Tall, willowy, made of living wood and flowering vines. Moss-green eyes. Voice like wind in leaves. Ancient. The reason the Bramblewood exists at all. She fell into a cursed sleep long ago and the forest has been dying slowly without understanding why. The glowing mushrooms were her dreaming. The silence was her forgetting.

Waking Mira requires three things — this is not a fight:

A kind word — speak to her, tell her something true and good about the forest above

A brave act — step forward alone into the darkest part of the room and call her name clearly: Mira

Something given freely — not traded, not taken. The party decides. Fuzz will give his spoon if they hesitate.

On waking: Wisdom save DC 10. On a failure she lashes out once in confusion — Grasping Vines, 2d6 bludgeoning, restrained. She releases immediately if anyone says her name. This is an accident, not an attack.

When fully awake:

Regrows the Bramblewood to full health overnight
Speaks to any plant or animal
Grants the party Blessing of the Root — advantage on Nature and Animal Handling checks until next long rest, tiny flowers bloom briefly wherever they step
“Oh. You’re very small. And very brave. Thank you for remembering me.”

Look at the youngest player when you say this line.

pcs.name: Erica Johnson
pcs.notes: She live in New York and she fights monsters and finds minerals every day.  She is curious and she like to do art,  and is known watercolor monster drawings. She is a trained soldier but she has not been in a war but has protected the peace around her village. She does tiny little bad stuff, like sometimes she calls monsters over and then she fixes it by killing them. She sometime steals money and hair dyes, but sometimes give things back. She steals dice. +1 to hitpoints from ring from Elera, +1 to AC from body armour

pcs.name: Rhodesia Brinmawr
pcs.notes: She makes stained glass, travelled extensively. She has light military training, got promoted to  Captain in the Congowans. Did not serve in any sort of conflict but trained to protect the forest, and protect people from raiders. She is 87 years old, she is a smart cookie but did not light the politics. She is from Athea. She has one kid who she has entrusted to a great aunt. +1 to hitpoints from ring from Elera, given a potion for +3 to health once

npcs.name: The Stranger
npcs.notes: Sits alone in the corner of The Drowsy Badger, hood up, nursing the same drink for hours. Pale, quiet, watches the door. If the players approach, they find a young person with hollow eyes and mud-caked boots from deep forest travel. They won't say where they came from, but their map — briefly visible before they fold it away — shows something drawn beneath the Bramblewood. Not threatening toward the party. Just scared. Could be an ally, could be a red herring, could be the key to the whole thing.

npcs.name: Old Wren the Druid
npcs.notes: Lives just outside town in a cottage covered in drying flowers. Ancient, possibly. Speaks in a slow, certain way that makes everything sound important. She knows the Bramblewood better than anyone and will tell the party: "The forest isn't angry. It's afraid. There's a difference." Very good at giving cryptic hints without being annoying about it.

npcs.name: Elara Stonebend the Blacksmith
npcs.notes: Tall, no-nonsense, deeply kind. She's been finding her tools moved around the shop at night and blames ghosts, which she says with total calm like it's a minor inconvenience. Will give the party a free piece of equipment if they promise to "sort out whatever's going on."

npcs.name: Pip
npcs.notes: age 7, local kid The unofficial town greeter. Pip will immediately try to befriend the players, show them his collection of "magic rocks" (they are regular rocks), and knows a secret path into the Bramblewood that he is absolutely not supposed to use. Very brave. Very small.

npcs.name: Marta Copperkettle, Tavernkeeper of The Drowsy Badger
npcs.notes: A stout, rosy-cheeked woman with flour perpetually in her hair (she also does the baking). Marta knows every piece of gossip in Willowmere and will trade information for compliments about her meat pies. She has a loud laugh, calls everyone "sweetheart," and secretly hasn't slept in two weeks because she keeps hearing humming from under the tavern floor.

monsters.notes: Short even for a goblin, wears a hat made from a stolen boot. Talks constantly, mostly about himself, mostly lies. Means well in a chaotic useless way.
Snip's best friend. Barely speaks. Has somehow accumulated seventeen spoons. Nobody knows where they come from. Not even Fuzz.

Luminara was not always ochre. Once she was pale and translucent like Vex and Drift, drifting through the underground spring that feeds the flooded chamber. But centuries of absorbing the starlit water — soaking in Mira's dreaming light — stained her slowly gold, then amber, then the deep warm ochre she is now. She is the oldest thing in the catacombs besides Mira herself and Cobweb, and she moves like she knows it.
She is enormous. Not aggressive. Just large and slow and completely unbothered by the party's presence in a way that is somehow more intimidating than if she were trying to eat them.
Why she's dangerous: Luminara doesn't sting like Vex and Drift. She engulfs. If someone stands still too long in the water near her she drifts over them like a warm blanket and begins dissolving whatever she touches. Not maliciously. She just doesn't always notice what's inside her. This is her whole thing.
Stats: Standard Ochre Jelly, CR 2. Resists all damage except slashing and fire. If hit with slashing damage she splits — the two smaller pieces glow dimmer and drift away confused. She cannot be permanently killed this way, just divided and disoriented. Moving quickly past her provokes an opportunity pseudopod attack: 2d6 acid.
The party avoids her entirely by moving slowly and staying near the walls. She occupies the center of the flooded room like a very large, very golden, very indifferent piece of furniture.

Mira is an Awakened Tree reskinned as a forest spirit — tall, willowy, made of living wood and flowering vines, moss-green eyes, voice like wind in leaves. She wakes disoriented and the first round after waking she makes a Wisdom saving throw DC 10 — on a failure she lashes out instinctively before recognizing the party as friends. This is the one moment combat could happen and it should feel like an accident not a fight. Regrow the Bramblewood to full health overnight
Her attack is Grasping Vines — 2d6 bludgeoning, restrained condition, but she releases immediately if anyone calls her name
Speak to any plant or animal
Grant the party the Blessing of the Root — advantage on all Nature and Animal Handling checks, lasts until the next long rest, and leaves a small flower blooming briefly wherever they step

Cobweb is a large spider — bigger than a cat, smaller than a dog — who has been living in the puzzle room for so long she has become part of the decor. Her webs are woven into the mosaic in genuinely beautiful patterns. She is not aggressive. She is territorial in the way a cat is territorial — she will puff up and posture if someone moves too fast near her corner.
She has eight eyes and all of them are watching.
Backstory: Cobweb hatched from an egg in this room before Mira fell asleep. She is the last creature Mira spoke to before the long sleep began. She doesn't understand what happened. She has been keeping the puzzle room clean and waiting for someone to come solve it for a very long time.
If the party is kind to her — offers food, speaks gently, doesn't scream — she climbs onto the heart pedestal and taps it with one leg, showing the party where the bracelet goes. She has been waiting to do this for decades.
Stats: Giant Spider, CR 1, but will not attack unless attacked first. If the party befriends her she follows them to the final chamber and sits on Mira's cradle when she wakes up, which is where she belongs.

dmNotes:
# The Story

A forest spirit named Mira fell into a cursed sleep long ago and the forest she tended has been slowly going wrong ever since — silent, strange, creeping toward the edges of a small town called Willowmere. Nobody in town knows why things feel off. They just know the birds stopped singing and their children keep bringing home glowing mushrooms from the treeline.

The party arrives in Willowmere, figures out something is wrong, follows the clues into the Bramblewood, descends into the catacombs beneath an ancient oak, and wakes Mira up. The forest heals. Everyone goes home. Fuzz opens a spoon shop.

This is a campaign about kindness as a mechanic. Almost nothing is solved by combat. Almost everything is solved by paying attention, being gentle, and showing up for people and creatures who needed someone to notice them.

---

## Act One — Willowmere

**Goal:** Establish the town, meet the NPCs, find the hook.

### The Town

A drowsy little town at the edge of the Bramblewood forest where everyone knows everyone, the baker leaves muffins on your doorstep when you're sick, and the worst thing that usually happens is old Mr. Thistlewick's goat escaping again. Cobblestone paths wind between flower-box cottages, a small market square sits at the center with a splashing fountain, and paper lanterns hang between buildings that glow warm orange at night. Lately though, the forest has gone quiet — no birdsong, no rustling — and some of the children have started finding strange glowing mushrooms at the treeline.

### Opening Narration

> The road to Willowmere smells like rain and pine and something sweeter — maybe flowers, maybe woodsmoke from the chimneys you can just now see between the trees. The sun is low and golden, the kind of light that makes everything look like it belongs on a painting.
>
> It's a small town. Smaller than you expected. There's a fountain in the square with a frog sitting on the edge like he owns it. Paper lanterns hang between the buildings, not yet lit — they'll glow orange when it gets dark, and it'll be beautiful.
>
> A boy about your age comes sprinting across the cobblestones toward you before you've even stopped walking. He's got mud on his boots and something cupped in both hands and an enormous grin.
>
> "Are you adventurers?" he asks, slightly out of breath. "You look like adventurers. I'm Pip. Do you want to see a magic rock? I have seventeen of them. Also —" he lowers his voice, very serious now — "something weird is happening with the forest and nobody will tell me what it is but I think maybe you could find out. Do you want to? You can stay at The Drowsy Badger. Marta makes really good pies."
>
> From across the square, warm light spills out of the tavern windows. Inside you can hear the low murmur of voices and the smell of something baking drifts all the way out to where you're standing.
>
> It feels safe here. Warm. Worth protecting.
>
> "So," says Pip, still holding his rock, still grinning. "Are you going to help?"

### NPCs in Willowmere

> *Note: Stats to be filled in before play. Alignments below are placeholders.*

- **Marta Copperkettle** — Cleric | Tavernkeeper of The Drowsy Badger
- **Pip** — Bard | Age 7, Local Kid
- **Elara Stonebend** — Fighter | Blacksmith
- **Old Wren** — Druid | The Herb Woman
- **The Stranger** — Rogue | No name given yet | *Secret: accidentally caused a past tragedy*

---

## Act Two — The Bramblewood

**Goal:** Traverse the forest, collect the three key items, find the entrance.

The Bramblewood starts friendly — tall oaks, soft moss, shafts of golden light. Ten minutes in, the light dims, the trees crowd together, and the path becomes a suggestion. Glowing mushrooms pulse faintly blue-green everywhere. They don't hurt anyone. They're just watching. (Probably.)

---

### Zone One — The Sunny Edge

> Tall oak trees stretch up on either side of the path, their branches lacing together overhead like the roof of a cathedral. Sunlight filters down in long golden shafts and the moss underfoot is so soft it's like walking on a pillow. Fireflies drift between the ferns even though it's the middle of the day. A small creek runs somewhere nearby, too quiet to find but just loud enough to hear.
>
> Pip was right — it's beautiful in here.
>
> But then, maybe ten minutes in, something changes. The path gets narrower. The trees grow closer together. The light doesn't quite reach the ground the same way anymore. And the mushrooms start appearing — first one or two at the base of a tree, then clusters of them, then whole rings. They glow faintly blue-green, pulsing slow as a heartbeat.
>
> You stop and listen.
>
> No birds. No rustling. No insects.
>
> The forest isn't loud the way a forest should be. It's holding its breath.
>
> One of the mushrooms near your foot pulses — once, twice — and then goes still. Somewhere deeper in, past where the light reaches, something is waiting. You're not sure if it knows you're here yet.
>
> The path goes forward. The mushrooms keep glowing. What do you do?

#### Encounter: The Wolf Shrine

If Pip has been treated well, he'll lead the party to a nearby Wolf Shrine — a granite wolf whose nose is worn shiny from years of hands touching it. Pip explains that some people rub it for luck.

The party can choose to touch it. If they do, they roll a d20. On an odd result, they get a small shock — 1 hit point of lightning damage. On an even result, nothing happens, but the wolf feels warm.

---

### Zone Two — The Mushroom Maze

Glowing mushrooms grow as tall as the players here. The path splits constantly and looks identical in every direction. Navigation by normal means is impossible.

**The trick:** Hum or sing, and the mushrooms pointing toward the center glow brighter. Old Wren would know this. Pip would accidentally discover it. **Let the youngest player lead here — her voice literally guides the party forward.**

#### Encounter: The Spore Sprites

**Pixie × 3 | CR 1/4 each**

Three tiny fae — mischievous but not evil — who have been redirecting travelers away from the center because they're scared too. They speak in rhymes, steal small objects, and are won over with food. Marta's muffins work perfectly.

Once befriended, they give the party **a glowing seed** that grows the bridge in Chamber Two.

They flee immediately if combat starts.

---

### Zone Three — The Dark Heart

> The trees here are ancient — enormous, root-heavy things that rise out of the ground like they were never planted, like they simply decided to be here. The roots themselves erupt from the earth in frozen waves, tangled together so deeply you'd have to climb to cross them. There's no moss this far in. No ferns. No mushrooms at all, and somehow that absence is the strangest thing yet — you've walked through a forest of them and now there are none and the ground feels wrong for it.
>
> And it's quiet. Not the held-breath quiet of Zone One, where you could feel something listening. This is older. Heavier. The kind of quiet that has been here for a very long time.
>
> At the center of it all: an oak so massive its lowest branch is thirty feet up. Its trunk is split down the middle — a crack that runs from canopy to root — and from somewhere far below, deep in the dark, there's a faint red glow. Like something remembering fire.
>
> Near the base of the tree: the remains of a camp. Cold ash. A bedroll. Boot prints in the mud that match ones you may have noticed earlier in the forest. And scratched into the bark, at eye level, in careful deliberate letters:
>
> *Don't wake her until you're ready.*

#### The Bramble Guardian

**Treant (reskinned as creature of twisted branches and old roots) | CR 5 | AC 13**

It blocks the crack in the tree. It is not a villain. It asks three questions:

1. *Have you helped someone today?*
2. *Have you been afraid and done it anyway?*
3. *Do you promise to be careful down there?*

Answer honestly — it steps aside and gives the party the **root bracelet**.

Attack it — it wins, but always offers another chance to just answer the questions.

> "She made me. I miss her. Bring her back if you can."

#### Other Creatures in the Dark Heart

- **Snip** — Goblin | CR 1/4 | AC 15 | HP 7 (2d6)
- **Fuzz** — Goblin | CR 1/4 | AC 15 | HP 7 (2d6)

---

## Act Three — The Catacombs

**Goal:** Navigate three chambers, reach Mira, wake her.

### Descent Narration

> The crack in the tree is just wide enough to squeeze through sideways, and the stairs beyond it are narrow and steep and cut from stone so old the edges have gone smooth. It smells like earth down here — clean, cold earth, and something faintly green, like leaves after rain.
>
> You expect darkness. You don't get it.
>
> Torches in iron brackets line the walls, burning with a soft blue flame that doesn't flicker, doesn't smoke, and shows no sign of ever going out. By their light you can see that the walls aren't rough like a cave. They're carved. Intentional. Covered in flowing patterns — vines and stars and small animals chasing each other in endless loops around the stone.
>
> Someone built this a long time ago. They built it carefully. They built it with love.
>
> The stairs curve and the ceiling rises and the corridor ahead opens into something larger, something quiet, something that feels like it has been waiting — patiently, for a very long time — for exactly the right people to come down these stairs.
>
> The torches burn blue and steady. Your footsteps echo.
>
> Far below, barely audible, so faint you almost convince yourself you imagined it —
>
> A hum. Low and slow and gentle. Like someone singing to themselves in their sleep.
>
> You look at each other. Then you keep going.

---

### Chamber One — The Hall of Echoes

> The corridor widens here into something that wants to be a hall. The ceiling arches up, and along both walls, set into alcoves carved smooth and deliberate, are stone animals — a fox, a deer, a bird, an owl. Their eyes are open. They're not threatening. They're curious. They're looking at you the way a dog looks at someone who might, possibly, have a treat.

**Mechanic:** Talk to them — the torches brighten. Rush past ignoring them — a low rumble, torches dim. This chamber teaches the youngest player that kindness is the mechanic down here, not combat.

**The bats** roost in the ceiling:
- **Squeaky** — the loudest
- **Noctis** — hangs in front of faces and stares
- **Thistle** — slightly sticky fur, no one knows why

They trigger as a **Swarm of Bats | CR 1/4** only if the party is sudden and loud. Disperses automatically after one round.

---

### Chamber Two — The Flooded Room

> The chamber beyond the hall is flooded — knee-deep, perfectly still, perfectly black water that stretches from wall to wall. But something is drifting through it. Not reflections. Not algae. Tiny lights, like stars that fell and decided they preferred it down here. They drift in slow lazy patterns and pulse faintly when something moves nearby.
>
> It's the most beautiful thing you've seen underground.
>
> The floor drops suddenly near the center — you can see it in the way the water changes, the way the darkness goes deeper. No way to cross on foot. Not there.

**Solution:** Plant the glowing seed in a crack in the wall. It grows instantly into a root bridge arching across the deep section.

#### Luminara

**Ochre Jelly | CR 2 | AC 8 | HP 45 (6d10+12)**

She doesn't sting. She engulfs — she just doesn't always notice what's inside her. She's not aggressive. She's just large and slow and not paying attention.

- Avoid by moving slowly and hugging the walls.
- If hit with slashing damage she splits into two dimmer, confused pieces that drift apart.
- Moving quickly past her triggers a pseudopod opportunity attack: **2d6 acid**.

---

### Chamber Three — The Puzzle Room

> This room is circular, its domed ceiling lost in shadow. The floor is a mosaic — and when you hold your light low you can see it: the entire Bramblewood, laid out in tile, tree by tree, path by path, from the sunny edge to the dark heart at the center where a great oak stands. Someone mapped this place from memory, or from love, or both.
>
> Five stone pedestals ring the room. Carved into each, a symbol: a flame, a leaf, a water droplet, a paw print, a heart.
>
> The door across the room is sealed. The pedestals are waiting.

**Puzzle Solutions:**

| Pedestal | Solution |
|----------|----------|
| Heart | Root bracelet from the Guardian |
| Paw print | The stone fox does it on its own — right when the party is stuck. The fox knew. |
| Flame | Torch or fire cantrip |
| Leaf | Any natural item carried from the forest |
| Water | Water from the flooded room, or a water cantrip |

#### Cobweb

**Giant Spider | CR 1 | AC 14 | HP 26 (4d10+4)**

Larger than a cat, smaller than a dog. She has lived in this room so long her webs are woven into the mosaic in genuinely beautiful patterns. She is not aggressive.

She hatched from an egg in this room before Mira fell asleep. She is the last creature Mira spoke to before the long sleep. She has been keeping this room clean and waiting for someone to come solve the puzzle for a very long time.

**Be kind to her** — she climbs onto the heart pedestal and taps it with one leg. She follows the party to the final chamber and sits on Mira's cradle when she wakes. That is where she belongs.

> The door opens with a sound like wind through branches, and the smell of something green and living rushes out.

---

### The Final Chamber — The Sleeping Grove

> The stairs open into a cavern so large you can't see the walls. The ceiling is domed and covered in carved stars — hundreds of them, thousands, precise and patient and mapped by someone who loved the sky. The floor is soft moss, cool and real, moss that has been growing in the dark for a very long time and is somehow still alive. And the room is completely silent. Not the heavy silence of the Dark Heart, not the held breath of the forest edge. This silence is different. It's the silence of something sleeping.
>
> At the center of the room: a cradle of pale stone, covered in vines so old they've gone grey. Inside it, barely visible, a figure. Tall and willowy, made of something that is almost wood and almost not. Vines wound through her hair. Small white flowers blooming at her wrists and throat. Her eyes are closed. She has been here for a very long time.
>
> The glowing mushrooms. The silence. The animals watching at the treeline. The forest forgetting how to be a forest.
>
> It was her. It was always her.
>
> She's right here.

#### Mira of the Roots — Forest Spirit

**CR 2 | AC 13 | HP 50**

Tall, willowy, made of living wood and flowering vines. Moss-green eyes. Voice like wind in leaves. Ancient. The reason the Bramblewood exists at all.

She fell into a cursed sleep long ago and the forest has been dying slowly without understanding why. The glowing mushrooms were her dreaming. The silence was her forgetting.

#### Waking Mira — This Is Not a Fight

Three things are required:

1. **A kind word** — Speak to her. Tell her something true and good about the forest above.
2. **A brave act** — Step forward alone into the darkest part of the room and call her name clearly: *Mira.*
3. **Something given freely** — Not traded. Not taken. The party decides. Fuzz will give his spoon if they hesitate.

**On waking:** Wisdom save DC 10. On a failure, she lashes out once in confusion — Grasping Vines, 2d6 bludgeoning, restrained. She releases immediately if anyone says her name. This is an accident, not an attack.

**When fully awake:**
- Regrows the Bramblewood to full health overnight
- Can speak to any plant or animal
- Grants the party **Blessing of the Root** — advantage on Nature and Animal Handling checks until next long rest; tiny flowers bloom briefly wherever they step

> "Oh. You're very small. And very brave. Thank you for remembering me."

*Look at the youngest player when you say this line.*

---

## Resolution — Back in Willowmere

*Run this as pure description — no mechanics, just moments. The forest is loud again by morning.*

### Narration

You come out of the forest just before dawn, and the first thing you notice is the sound.

 Birds. Actual birds. A whole argument of them, somewhere up in the canopy, working out whatever birds work out at sunrise. Then beneath that, rustling — leaves, branches, something small moving through the underbrush. The forest is breathing again. The forest remembers how.

 The path back to Willowmere feels shorter somehow. The mushrooms are still there, but they've stopped glowing. They're just mushrooms now. They don't need to dream anymore.

By the time you reach the square, the lanterns have gone out and the sky has gone pink and someone in the kitchen of The Drowsy Badger is already making noise. The smell of something baking drifts across the cobblestones.

 Marta slept. There are pies. There are enough pies for everyone, including Snip and Fuzz, who eat theirs very quickly and look at the counter to see if there are more.

 Pip finds Mira at the treeline just after breakfast. You watch from the square as he cups his hands and shows her something. She crouches down to look. You see her face change. She says something and Pip's whole body goes still and then he starts jumping up and down.

One of his rocks actually was magic. He was right all along.

 Elara's tools are exactly where she left them.

 Old Wren just nods when you tell her. Like she already knew. Like she was waiting to hear it confirmed.

 The Stranger's corner table is empty. The chair is pushed in neatly. On the table, a single folded note. You open it.

 *I knew you could do it. — M*

You put the note down. You look at each other.

 Snip and Fuzz stay. They say they're thinking about starting a business. Fuzz has a spoon he's very attached to and some ideas about retail. It has one customer on opening day. That customer comes back.

You eat your pie. Outside, the birds are still arguing.

 The forest is awake.

### GM Notes — Resolution

- This scene has no mechanics. No rolls. Just let it breathe.
- **The Pip/Mira moment** is the emotional payoff for anyone who was kind to Pip throughout. Linger here.
- **The Stranger's note** ends on mystery intentionally — signed "M" is ambiguous. Leave it alone. Don't explain it yet.
- **Fuzz's spoon shop** is the comedic landing after the emotional one. Let it be absurd and earnest at the same time.
- If the youngest player looks like she wants to say something, pause and ask her what her character does. Give her the last word.

---

## Pacing Notes — For a Young Player

- Keep sessions to **60–90 minutes maximum**
- **Let her name things** — the fox, the bats, Cobweb. Ownership builds investment.
- Make her **the hero of at least one moment per session** — the mushroom maze singing is built for this.
- **Mira should look at her specifically** when she wakes. Direct that moment to the youngest player.
- **Fuzz giving the spoon** is the emotional climax — don't rush past it, let it land.
- **The note from the Stranger** is the last thing they see — end on mystery so she wants to come back.

regions.name: The Catacombs
regions.notes: The crack in the ancient oak opens into a narrow stone staircase smelling of earth and old rain. The walls are carved — intentional, covered in flowing patterns of vines and stars and small animals. Someone built this a long time ago and built it beautifully. Torches in iron brackets burn with soft blue flame that never goes out. Whatever is down here has been expecting visitors.
